sourcePolicy.policy: Recent scholars are summarized as short, source-linked scholarly lenses. No copyrighted source text is reproduced. relationType distinguishes direct commentary, methodology, background, reception history, and contextual application.

sources.sourceTitle: Immigration and Apocalypse: How the Book of Revelation Shaped American Immigration
sources.basis: Yale University Press description and Yale Divinity profile/CV
sources.use: Revelation, apocalyptic rhetoric, migration, exclusion, New Jerusalem as political myth

sources.sourceTitle: Archaeology and the Letters of Paul
sources.use: Pauline letters, archaeology, gender, race, status, power, colonialism, social/economic/religious context

sources.sourceTitle: Divine Accounting: Theo-Economics in Early Christianity
sources.basis: Yale University Press description and recent reviews
sources.use: economic language, debt, gift, exchange, divine-human economy, Philippians/Pauline theo-economics

sources.sourceTitle: The Embodied God: Seeing the Divine in Luke-Acts and the Early Church
sources.basis: Duke Divinity book notice and OUP description
sources.use: Luke-Acts, embodiment, visibility of God, divine/human bodies, healing and vision narratives
slavery, leadership, unfree status, Pauline assemblies, Philemon, household codes

sources.sourceTitle: African American Readings of Paul: Reception, Resistance, and Transformation
sources.use: Pauline reception, African American interpretive history, resistance and transformation

sources.sourceTitle: If God Still Breathes, Why Can't I?: Black Lives Matter and Biblical Authority
sources.use: womanist biblical authority, whiteness, contextual theology, Black Lives Matter and biblical interpretation

sources.sourceTitle: God's Ghostwriters: Enslaved Christians and the Making of the Bible
sources.use: enslaved scribes, messengers, co-laborers, erased labor in New Testament production
sources.caution: claims include speculative elements; use as contested related perspective

sources.scholar: Erin Vearncombe / Brandon Scott / Hal Taussig / Westar Institute
sources.use: first two centuries, Jesus movements, plurality before later institutional Christianity

notes: Modern copyrighted sources are represented as short paraphrased interpretive lenses, not copied source text. Yale-related additions include Yii-Jan Lin and Laura Salah Nasrallah, with Jennifer Quigley from Yale University Press also included for theo-economics.
